skill.name: run-train
skill.description: Conservative training command executor for deep learning research repositories. It captures startup checks, short runs, full kickoffs, and resume runs with structured evidence in train_outputs/.
skill.summary: Run a selected training command and collect reproducible training evidence.
skill.author: lllllllama

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed in context. Markdown backticks, relative documentation paths, natural-language crypto terms, and the system reconnaissance flag are false positives, but the skill intentionally executes user-selected training commands and loads a shared writer module. No prompt injection, network exfiltration, credential harvesting, or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: User-Selected Training Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The runner executes the selected training command with subprocess.run. This is the stated purpose of the skill and uses shlex.split, capture_output, timeouts, and a working-directory constraint, but it still runs arbitrary user-provided process arguments in the target repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is supplied through --command and passed to subprocess.run after shlex.split. The behavior is intentional and bounded, but it is a real external execution capability.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Shared Writer Module Loading
medium_findings.description: The output wrapper loads a Python module from a computed shared scripts path and executes it with exec_module. This appears to be a local shared helper dependency, not malicious behavior, but it expands trust to code outside this skill file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic import and module execution are explicit. The path is deterministic and local, so the risk is dependency trust rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Git Status Commands
low_findings.description: The runner calls git through subprocess.run to collect repository status before and after training. Arguments are fixed by the script, so the static external-command finding at this location does not show command injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The git executable and argument lists are constructed from hardcoded values. The repository path is user supplied, but it is used as cwd rather than interpolated into a shell command.

dangerous_patterns.title: Confirmed External Command Execution With Bounded Controls
dangerous_patterns.description: subprocess.run is used for hardcoded git probes and for the selected training command. The git probes are false positives for injection, while the training command execution is a legitimate but sensitive core capability that requires user trust.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact subprocess calls are present at the flagged lines. Context shows no shell=True use, but line 164 still executes user-selected arguments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static False Positive: Markdown Backticks
dangerous_patterns.description: The Ruby or shell backtick findings are markdown inline-code markers in SKILL.md and references/training-policy.md. They are documentation formatting, not executable Ruby or shell command substitution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged files are markdown or YAML text, and the backticks delimit inline code names. No interpreter executes these characters.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static False Positive: Documentation Path References
dangerous_patterns.description: The path traversal findings are relative links to shared reference files in SKILL.md. They are not used by code to open, read, or write arbitrary paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both findings are in prose documentation lines that name reference files. No filesystem API call appears at those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Terms
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptography findings match natural language and training terminology, including summary text, checkpoint wording, and an argparse description. No cryptographic function, hashing routine, or credential protection logic is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain descriptions or training workflow language. No evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar algorithm use was found in the inspected code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static False Positive: System Reconnaissance Heading
dangerous_patterns.description: The system reconnaissance finding is a markdown section heading and avoid list in references/training-policy.md. It does not inspect host details, enumerate files, or collect system information.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The content is plain policy text listing behaviors to avoid. There are no commands or APIs for reconnaissance at the flagged location.

content.value_statement: Deep learning runs often lack clear status and reproducible evidence. This skill runs one selected command and records structured training outputs.
actual_capabilities: Runs one selected training command with a configurable timeout.
Supports startup verification, short-run verification, full kickoff, and resume modes.
Captures standard output and standard error from the training process.
Extracts visible epoch, step, metric, and checkpoint hints from logs.
Records git status differences before and after the run.
Produces standardized training evidence under train_outputs/.
limitations: It does not install dependencies or download datasets.
It does not choose research goals, models, or training strategy.
It is not designed for exploratory sweeps or multiple variants.
Its conclusions are limited by the configured monitoring window.

use_cases.title: Verify a training startup
use_cases.description: Run a short launch check and record whether the selected command starts cleanly.

use_cases.title: Resume an interrupted run
use_cases.description: Continue from a known checkpoint and capture status, metrics, and checkpoint evidence.

use_cases.title: Kick off a documented run
use_cases.description: Start a selected full training command and collect bounded monitoring evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Run startup verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Run startup verification for this selected training command with a 60 second monitoring window. Record the command, logs, status, and any early metric evidence.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check before a longer training run

prompt_templates.title: Run a short verification window
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the selected training command in short-run verification mode for 120 seconds. Capture completed steps, visible metrics, checkpoints, and stop reason.
prompt_templates.scenario: Confirm that training progresses beyond startup

prompt_templates.title: Resume from a checkpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Resume the selected training command from the provided checkpoint. Mark the run as resume mode and record checkpoint, metric, and git status evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Launch a full kickoff
prompt_templates.prompt: Launch the selected full training command with a bounded monitoring window. Write the standard train_outputs evidence bundle and report any blockers.

output_examples.input: Startup verification request for a selected training command.
output: A summary states whether startup succeeded, failed, or timed out.
A command record lists the selected run mode and exact command context.
A log file includes captured standard output and standard error.
A status report names the stop reason and visible progress evidence.

output_examples.input: Resume request from a known checkpoint.
output: The report identifies the resume source and the latest visible checkpoint.
The status notes completed steps when they appear in the logs.
The evidence bundle separates successful resume evidence from partial progress.

output_examples.input: Full kickoff request with a monitoring window.
output: The report explains whether the command completed or exceeded the monitoring window.
The logs preserve visible metrics and checkpoint hints.
The file-change summary lists changed, new, and deleted repository paths.

best_practices: Provide a selected, reviewed training command before invoking the skill.
Set a timeout that matches the expected early progress window.
Review the captured file changes before treating the run as reproducible evidence.
anti_patterns: Using this skill to install dependencies or download assets.
Running exploratory sweeps or many training variants through this skill.
Treating startup verification as proof of full reproduction success.

faq.question: What run modes does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports startup verification, short-run verification, full kickoff, and resume modes.

faq.question: Where does it write evidence?
faq.answer: It writes standardized evidence under train_outputs/ in the target repository.

faq.question: Does it choose the training command?
faq.answer: No. The skill expects a documented or selected command before execution.

faq.question: Can it run hyperparameter sweeps?
faq.answer: No. It is scoped to one selected command, not exploratory sweeps or variants.

faq.question: What happens when the timeout is reached?
faq.answer: The run is reported as partial, with the monitoring window recorded as the stop reason.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The skill intentionally runs external training commands, so users must review commands before execution.
